text.title: On-Call Rotation Setup Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of on-call-rotation-setup enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Shift length is bounded — typical 8-24h depending on load; >24h requires medical / safety review per EU WTD.
rule.rationale: Long shifts compound fatigue and cause incident-causing oversight; regulatory exposure for unbounded shifts.

rule.statement: Mission-critical services have primary + secondary tiers; primary tries first, secondary escalated after defined SLA.
rule.rationale: Single-tier rotations have no failure mode for primary being unreachable.

rule.statement: On-call is compensated — explicit pay rate OR time-off-in-lieu OR equivalent; uncompensated on-call is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Uncompensated on-call masks the true cost and accelerates burnout.

rule.statement: Fairness is audited quarterly: per-engineer page count + shift count + weekend ratio. Outliers trigger rebalance.
rule.rationale: Unaudited rotations drift toward 'whoever doesn't complain' carrying the load.

rule.statement: Rotations have a 'safety valve' — engineers can swap shifts with peers without management approval if both agree.
rule.rationale: Bureaucratic swaps cause attendance gaps; trusted-peer swaps preserve coverage.

rule.statement: If preconditions in `Applies If` are not all true, skip this methodology and route to the adjacent one referenced in Related.
rule.rationale: A skip rule keeps the decision tree closed: every branch ends at a real rule id.
